name: B.A. in Interior Design

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The interior-design course catalog is coherent and professionally structured: a studio ladder INT 263 -> 264 -> 363 -> 364 -> 464 (deepestPrereqChain 5) runs alongside Building and Interior Systems I->II (INT 221/222), Structural Principles (INT 223), Methods and Materials (INT 321), Contract Documents (INT 322), and History of Furniture (INT 350). A real coherence problem, however, is that the parsed plan of study populates the studio slots with the Industrial Design studios (ID 263/264/363/364/464) rather than these INT studios, and the First Year is missing entirely, so the plan as shown does not match the interior-design course spine.
dimensions.suggestion: Correct the plan of study to reference the INT studio sequence (INT 263-464) rather than the ID studios, and add the missing First Year so the interior-design progression is accurate and complete.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is healthy: missingCourseCount is only 2 (GER 300 and the PHYS 102A lab), and the INT chains are sensible (INT 264 <- AD 150, INT 263; INT 364 <- INT 221, INT 222, INT 321, INT 363; INT 464 <- ARCH 282, ARCH 337, INT 321, INT 322, INT 364). The deepest chain of 5 is reasonable. One data flaw: INT 263's description carries a copy-paste restriction 'For Industrial Design majors only.'
dimensions.suggestion: Register PHYS 102A and GER 300, and fix the INT 263 course note that erroneously restricts an interior-design studio to Industrial Design majors.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives appear in the data. Interior design is a professionally accredited field (CIDA/NCIDQ), so the absence of stated outcomes around code-compliant, human-centered interior environments is a notable catalog gap.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 5-8 program learning outcomes aligned to professional interior-design competencies (space planning, codes and life safety, materials and finishes, building systems, professional practice).

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: creditReconcileDelta is -31 (summedTermCredits 89 vs. stated 120), consistent with the First Year (~31 credits) being absent from the parsed plan; this is largely a data-completeness caveat rather than a genuine imbalance. Across the six parsed terms (Years 2-4) the load is steady at 14-17 credits, but with the First Year missing and several credit-less GER rows, the full gen-ed/core split cannot be verified.
dimensions.suggestion: Add the First-Year terms so the plan reconciles to 120 credits and assign explicit credits to the null GER rows so the distribution is transparent.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Years 2-4 are labeled with per-term credits, but the page has several clarity problems: the First Year is missing, the studio rows reference ID studios instead of the program's own INT studios, GER rows appear with null credits (Science GER, Social Sciences GER, senior-seminar GER), and 'or R830 101' alternatives appear as separate credit-less rows. No program description is present.
dimensions.suggestion: Rebuild the plan with the correct INT studios and the First Year, fold the 'or' alternatives into their parent rows, credit the null GER items, and add a program overview.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The curriculum is strongly aligned with professional interior-design practice: Building and Interior Systems I-II (INT 221/222) cover HVAC/lighting/acoustics, INT 321 addresses code requirements and life safety, INT 322 Contract Documents covers professional documentation, ID 341 adds sustainable materials, and ARCH 337 Building Information Modeling is a prerequisite of the INT 464 capstone. Currency gaps are targeted rather than broad: no dedicated lighting-design course, no explicit universal/inclusive-design or accessibility course, and no wellness-certification (WELL) content.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a dedicated lighting-design course and an accessibility/universal-design offering, and introduce WELL/wellness-standard content to match current practice.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are detailed and profession-specific (INT 221/222 explain large- and small-scale interior building systems; INT 321 covers code requirements, life safety, and specification; INT 350 surveys furniture history and its social/design forces; INT 464 describes a comprehensive multi-level institutional studio). None read as thin; the main flaw is a mis-copied restriction line in INT 263, not a lack of content.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Most prerequisites are explicit and well-formed (INT 464 <- ARCH 282, ARCH 337, INT 321, INT 322, INT 364; INT 264 <- AD 150, INT 263). Clarity is reduced by a couple of ambiguous multi-operator strings (INT 321's 'AD 111, AD 112, AD 150 or ARCH 334, AD 161, AD 162 and ARCH 251') and by the plan-of-study/course mismatch where ID studios stand in for the INT studios the prerequisites actually reference.

missingTopics.rationale: Building Systems (INT 221/222) touch systems broadly, but interior design requires a dedicated lighting course (daylighting, layered electric lighting, controls) that the plan does not isolate.
INT 321 covers code requirements in part, but a standalone building-codes and ADA/universal-design course is expected for NCIDQ-track interior programs.
Comfort and acoustic performance are core to interior environments yet are not addressed as a distinct topic beyond the general systems courses.
ARCH 337 Building Information Modeling is a prerequisite of INT 464 but appears only as a gateway; an interiors-focused BIM/documentation course would make digital delivery an explicit competency.

missingTopics.topic: Furniture, fixtures, and equipment (FF&E) specification and estimating
missingTopics.rationale: INT 321 Methods and Materials and INT 350 History of Furniture provide context, but a dedicated FF&E specification/estimating course reflects a central professional deliverable.

emergingTrends.rationale: Health-focused interior standards (WELL) and biophilic design are reshaping the field; the human-factors (AD 201) and systems courses are natural anchors to add this content.
The INT 364 studio already spans healthcare and institutional types, positioning the program to formalize evidence-based design methods.
ID 341 Sustainable Materials is a foothold; interiors practice increasingly requires certification literacy and circular material strategies.
With ARCH 337 BIM already gating the capstone, the program is well placed to make model-based interior documentation a through-line.

emergingTrends.trend: Adaptive reuse and flexible/hybrid workplace design
emergingTrends.rationale: Post-pandemic reprogramming of offices and adaptive reuse are major market drivers the commercial-focused INT 364/464 studios could target explicitly.

topSuggestions: Correct the plan of study to reference the program's own INT studio sequence (INT 263-464) instead of the Industrial Design studios, and add the missing First Year so the plan reconciles to 120 credits.
Publish explicit, accreditation-aligned program learning outcomes (none are currently present).
Add a dedicated lighting-design course and a codes/life-safety/accessibility (ADA/universal design) course to meet professional-track expectations.
Introduce WELL/wellness and sustainability-certification content, building on ID 341 and AD 201, to match current interior-design practice.
Fix the data flaws: the INT 263 note that wrongly restricts the studio to Industrial Design majors, the null-credit GER rows, and the 2 dangling references (GER 300, PHYS 102A).
